summary: After World War II and the internment, I lived in Beacon Hill, Seattle around 1959. My living situation was modest, with about $75 a month and no real estate of my own. Eventually, after resettling elsewhere, I returned to Seattle once things had settled to avoid difficulties. Beacon Hill was part of my post-war life as I rebuilt and planned for my family's future.

Life during the Great Depression was tough, especially raising children with inadequate income. Business was slow, and many families struggled to make ends meet. This period shaped much of my early experience in America before the war changed everything again.

During World War II, Japanese people like myself were treated as enemies and faced a lot of hatred. I was too shocked to feel much at first when the evacuation orders came. The war changed my life drastically, leading to internment and a very uncertain future.

The Japanese American internment was a very difficult time. We learned about the evacuation through newspapers, radio, and notices on utility poles. I sold my business and possessions, taking only the minimum allowed, and was relocated to places like the Fuyallup Center and Minidoka in Idaho. Life in the camps was harsh but not much different from outside, and I tried to stay loyal to America despite the circumstances.

Although not deeply detailed, Fife, Washington was mentioned as part of the broader context of my experiences. It was near where some of the internment assembly centers were located, such as the Fuyallup Center. This area was part of the early phase of relocation during the internment period.

Minidoka in Idaho was one of the main internment camps where I was relocated during World War II. The camp was makeshift, with barns and a converted stadium used for housing. Life there was difficult but I engaged in activities like gardening and playing Go to keep my spirits up.

After leaving the camps, I resettled on the outskirts of Chicago, Illinois. My uncle was already working in greenhouses there, which helped me find a job in the same field. Life was financially tough, but I focused on securing employment and education for my son during this time.

Working in a greenhouse was my first job after resettling in Chicago. My uncle was already employed in greenhouses, which made it easier for me to find work there. This job was crucial as I tried to rebuild our lives after the internment and focus on my family's future.
